additionalTime: 10 minutes cooling time plus 20 minutes flavor rest
author: Robert Carr
description: Creamy jalapeno taqueria salsa made by cooking jalapenos and garlic, then blending them with neutral oil, lime, a little vinegar, cilantro, and salt.
ingredients: 1/2 cup avocado oil or other neutral oil
1 1/4 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt, or 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/4 cup cilantro leaves and tender stems, optional
2 to 4 tablespoons jalapeno cooking water, only as needed
instructions: Place 10 stemmed jalapenos and 4 peeled garlic cloves in a medium saucepan and cover with water by 1 inch.
Bring to a simmer over medium-high heat and cook for 8 to 10 minutes, until the jalapenos turn olive-green and soften.
Use a slotted spoon to transfer the jalapenos and garlic to a plate and let them cool for 10 minutes. Reserve the cooking water.
For a milder sauce, split the cooked jalapenos and remove some or all of the seeds before blending.
Add the cooled jalapenos, garlic, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 1 teaspoon white vinegar, 1 1/4 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/4 cup cilantro if using to a blender.
Blend until the jalapenos are mostly broken down.
With the blender running, slowly stream in 1/2 cup avocado oil through the lid opening, then blend on high for 20 to 30 seconds until the salsa turns pale and creamy.
If the salsa is too thick to pour, blend in reserved jalapeno cooking water 1 tablespoon at a time until it is spoonable and lightly drippy.
Taste and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt or a few drops of lime juice.
Let the salsa rest for at least 20 minutes before serving.
notes: This salsa is creamy because the cooked jalapenos blend smoothly with oil. It does not contain dairy.
A high-powered blender such as a Vitamix 5200 makes the smoothest texture, but a regular blender works if you stream the oil slowly and blend a little longer.
Keep the acid restrained. Too much lime or vinegar makes creamy jalapeno salsa taste sharp instead of green and rich.
Avocado oil keeps the flavor clean. Canola or grapeseed oil also works. Olive oil is not ideal here because it can turn bitter and assertive when blended hard.
For a mild version, remove most of the jalapeno seeds after cooking. For a hotter version, leave the seeds in or replace 2 jalapenos with serranos.
Do not add the oil all at once. Streaming it in helps the oil blend into the sauce instead of floating on top.
This salsa works especially well on breakfast burritos, tacos, potatoes, grilled chicken, eggs, quesadillas, and rice bowls.
title: Creamy Jalapeño Taqueria Salsa
